coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots with dual emission and their biological and sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Waheed Ullah
Zhou, Ping
Qin, Liying
Alam, Abid
Ge, Zhangjie
Wang, Yuhua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots with dual emission have drown increasing attention in biology and medicine due to their fascinating optical and biological properties. Herein, dual emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) are synthesized by simple annealing of ammonium citrate in the air via solvent-free carbonization. Interestingly, N-doped CDs display dual emission peaking at 462 nm and 560 nm under excitation of 360 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The dual PL emission spectra of the N-doped CDs illustrate that each emission is associated with the single excitation band. The data reveals that two autonomous fluorescent centres are co-existed in N-doped CDs because doping with nitrogen (N) could change the surface chemistry of the CDs. In addition, dual emissive N-doped CDs have significantly low toxicity and could be applied as multicolor cell imaging using T-ca cancer cells. Moreover, obtained N-doped CDs are sensitive to temperature in the range of 15–90 °C and show potential to detect temperature in living cells. Furthermore, low toxicity and high biocompatibility proved that the obtained N-doped CDs could be applied in vivo imaging. Besides, the dual emissive N-doped CDs have the ability to probe Fe3+ ions in water as well as in living T-ca cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual fluorescence
High bio-compatibility
Temperature-dependent emission
Multicolor imaging
Fe3+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of dual emissive N-doped CDs
Possible mechanism of dual emissive N-doped CDs
Applications of dual emissive N-doped CDs
Long-term intercellular stability of N-doped CDs in T-ca cells
N-doped CDs optical response to Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical description of dual emission N-doped CDs using solvent-free carbonization method.
(a) TEM and (inset) HRTEM image and (b) particle size histogram of N-doped CDs.
(a) Wide XPS spectrum of N-doped CDs. High-resolution survey scan spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
(a) Optical UV–Vis spectrum; inset the absorption spectrum is ranging from 400 to 600 nm. (b) Photo-luminescent excitation spectra of N-doped CDs monitoring at 462 and 560 nm emission wavelengths.

(a) Photographs of N-doped CDs solution excited with day-light, 365 nm UV lamp and 450 nm blue LED lamp (left to right); (b) PL excitation dependent emission of N-doped CDs from 300 nm to 520 nm excitation.
Proposed oxygen and nitrogen states diagram of N-doped CDs.

T-ca cells after 4 h treatment with N-doped CDs. These pictures are captured under (a) 406 nm excitation. (b) 488 nm excitation and (c) 561 nm excitation. The images named (d–f) are the merged images of (a–c). All scale bars are 50 μm.

Dual emissive N-doped CDs performance in T-ca cells under confocal microscopy. (a–d) 4 h, (e–h) 1 day, (i–l) 4 days and (m–p) 8 days. Blue, green and red channels are excited by 406 nm 488 nm and 561 nm, respectively. All scale bars are 100 μm.
(a) PL spectra (cyan and yellow) at different experimental temperatures ranging from 15 to 90 °C. (b) PL peak intensity is plotted versus temperature for cyan and yellow emission.
Temperature dependent imaging of T-ca cell under excitation of 406 nm, 488 nm, and 561 with temperature (a–c) 25 °C. (d–f) 35 °C, and (g–i) 45 °C. All scale bars are 100 μm.

(a) Kunming mice H&E-stained tissues slices (Heart, Liver, Spleen, Lung, Kidney) in PBS and N-doped CDs after 30 days. Nude mice treated with N-doped CDs by subcutaneous injection and observed the mice under different temperatures (b) 27 °C (c) 35 °C (d) 45 °C.

Fluorescence response of the dual emissive N-doped CDs in the presence of metal ions, where f and f0 are the corresponding fluorescent intensities in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively (a and b) excited by 360 nm and 470 nm. (c and d) N-doped CDs (Cyan and yellow emission) emission spectra with various concentrations of Fe3+ range from 0 μM to 300 μM. (e and f) PL intensity (f/f0) was plotted against the various concentrations of Fe3+ for cyan emission and yellow emission.
Reported CDs as a Fe3+ probe.

head.ce:title: Solvent-free synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots with dual emission and their biological and sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of Optical-Conversion Materials and Technology & School of Materials and Energy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of Optical-Conversion Materials and Technology & School of Materials and Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory of Optical-Conversion Materials and Technology & School of Materials and Energy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study and School of Physics and Optical Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Advanced Study and School of Physics and Optical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study and School of Physics and Optical Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Stomatology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Stomotology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with dual emission have drown increasing attention in biology and medicine due to their fascinating optical and biological properties. Herein, dual emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped CDs) are synthesized by simple annealing of ammonium citrate in the air via solvent-free carbonization. Interestingly, N-doped CDs display dual emission peaking at 462 nm and 560 nm under excitation of 360 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The dual PL emission spectra of the N-doped CDs illustrate that each emission is associated with the single excitation band. The data reveals that two autonomous fluorescent centres are co-existed in N-doped CDs because doping with nitrogen (N) could change the surface chemistry of the CDs. In addition, dual emissive N-doped CDs have significantly low toxicity and could be applied as multicolor cell imaging using T-ca cancer cells. Moreover, obtained N-doped CDs are sensitive to temperature in the range of 15–90 °C and show potential to detect temperature in living cells. Furthermore, low toxicity and high biocompatibility proved that the obtained N-doped CDs could be applied in vivo imaging. Besides, the dual emissive N-doped CDs have the ability to probe Fe3+ ions in water as well as in living T-ca cells.

ce:section.ce:para: W.U. Khan design the experiments and wrote the manuscript. W.U. Khan and A. Alam characterized the material. P. Zhou and L. Qin design and perform the biological experiments. Z. Ge and L. Qin perform the vivo-imaging experiment. W. Yuhua revise the manuscript and supervise the project.

Recently, luminescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant interest in the field of bio-imaging, chemical and biosensors, photo-catalysis, drugs and genes delivery, and photovoltaic applications due to their promising physico-chemical properties such as strong absorbance and photo-luminescence (PL), excellent water solubility, resistance to photo-bleaching, non/low toxicity, low cost, and having various synthetic procedures [1–7]. Up to now, large numbers of synthetic methods have been developed to synthesize CDs, and these methods can be generally divided into two categories such as physical and chemical approaches [8,9]. In the physical approaches, the bigger pieces of materials are breakdown into the desire nano-size by arc discharge method, laser ablation, and plasma treatment [10–12]. In the chemical approaches, the nano-particles are obtained from assembling molecular precursors/biomass under different reaction conditions, including hydro/solvo-thermal, chemical or electrochemical oxidation, ultrasonic, microwave-assisted conditions and solvent-free carbonization method [11,13–18]. The methods mention above requires large amount of solvent as reaction medium that might be expansive, flammable, toxic, and high pressure of the solvents reaction in enclosed system is always a danger for large-scale production. Particularly, the latest method (solvent-free carbonization) is advantageous due to its one step facile preparation, high-production yield, solvent free, excellent water solubility, low cost, and ability to generate a verity of CDs.

Dual or tunable emission is considered to be the most fascinating feature of CDs [19–22]. Many efforts have been made to develop dual or multicolor emissive CDs, however, the majority of the CDs reported exhibit strong emission peaks in the blue light region, which is hindering their bio-medical applications because of the auto-fluorescence of bio-system and UV damaging [23,24]. The excitation-dependent emission phenomenon has been often observed in CDs, whereas there is a major decrease recorded in the fluorescent intensity upon varying the excitation wavelength [1,25–29]. Recently, some reported CDs show dual or multiple emissions ranging from blue to red light region, but they contain various reagents (carbon, nitrogen, sulfur sources), involving toxic components or organic solvents, poor water solubility, need long reaction time under high temperature and pressure and the resulting CDs suffer from low-production yields [30–33]. The ability of freely tunable/dual fluorescence behavior of CDs is preferable for many practical applications, but this tunability is rarely achieved. Therefore, preparing N-doped CDs from a single precursor having dual emissions through facile one-step method with considerable intensities is still a big challenge in modern nano-science.

In this work, solvent-free carbonization method has been developed as a powerful synthetic tool to synthesize dual emissive nitrogen doped CDs by using a single precursor ammonium citrate, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Interestingly, the emission peaks of the N-doped CDs are tuned from cyan to yellow with a maximum quantum yield of 12.6% and 7.8%, respectively. To best in my knowledge, it is the first report using single compound for obtaining N-doped CDs with dual emission and 43% of production yield. The possible mechanism explains that the dual emission comes from two independent fluorescent centres trigged by specific wavelength light. Specifically, the cyan emission could come from C=O transition and yellow light could come from C=N transition. TEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, and Raman shift are used to characterize the obtained dual emissive N-doped CDs. Optical properties of the emissive product (N-doped CDs) are recorded by acquiring UV–Visible absorption and PL spectrometer. Obtained N-doped CDs are utilized for sensing and biological applications.

Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company supplied the ammonium citrate (C6H17O7N3), ethanol, and all salts. Chloral hydrate was purchased from Aladdin. All the reagents are of analytical grade and have not been purified further.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dual emissive N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: One-step solvent-free carbonization method has been chosen for dual emissive N-doped CDs from single ammonium citrate without any additive passivating agent/acids/alkali/catalyst or organic solvents. Generally, ammonium citrate of 0.3 g was ground in agate mortar very gently. Afterward, the obtained product is put in an electric oven and the temperature has been set at 180 °C for 1 h. After the reaction time is over, the oven is cooled down naturally. The obtained product (0.015 g) was dispersed in 20 mL of water and centrifuge at 12,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the large particles. The solution is further purified by a filter membrane (0.22 μm) for future experiments.

The morphology and hydrodynamic size distribution histogram of the N-doped CDs are presented in Fig. 2 . The particles possess a nearly spherical shape and uniformly spread in the TEM image, as shown in Fig. 2a. The particular high-resolution (HR) TEM image is given in the inset (Fig. 2a and Fig. S1) demonstrates that the N-doped CDs exhibit partially crystalline structure. The crystalline and amorphous portion (yellow dotted line) of the N-doped CDs is clearly displayed in inset Fig. 2a. The well-resolved lattice fringes of face distance (d = 0.204 nm) are recorded, which is similar to (101) plane of graphitic carbons [34]. The hydrodynamic particle size of the N-doped CDs is distinct and varied from 2 nm to 13 nm with the average size of approximately ∼6.8 nm (Fig. 2b). XRD curve of the dual emissive N-doped CDs shows a broad diffraction peak located at 2θ = 25.6° (Fig. S2), which is well-matched with (002) facet, indicating highly disordered carbons. The results from (HR)TEM combines with XRD curve demonstrate that the dual emissive N-doped CDs are crystalline carbon core with amorphous functional groups on the surface [35]. The Raman shift characteristics are hidden in the measurement (Fig. S3) because the nano-crystalline core of N-doped CDs is covered by sp3 amorphous states [36,37]. The Raman shift observation is well-matched with the XRD and HR (TEM) results.

XPS elemental analysis (Fig. 3 a and Table S1) clearly illustrates that 3 main components (C1s, N1s, and O1s) of the CDs are existed on the surface with atomic concentration of 66.81%, 11.56%, and 21.64%, respectively. The carbon content is larger (66.81%), indicating that the N-doped CDs are primarily based on carbon. High-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) could be fitted into three peaks with binding energy of 284.4, 285.4, and 288.1 (eV), which corresponds to C1s in the states of C=C, C–N, and C=O, respectively [38]. The surface environment of the obtained CDs has a nitrogen content of 11.56%, which is further illustrated that the obtained CDs are nitrogen-rich and strongly doped with N. As confirm from Fig. 3c, the N1s spectra display two peaks, which are centred at 399.5 and 400.2 (eV), assigning to N1s in the states of amino N and pyrrolic N, respectively [39]. Fig. 3(a and d) show that the obtained N-doped CDs is also oxygen-rich with the evidence by the fact that 21.64% of (O1s) is presented on the surface. The binding energy values for C=O and C–OH/C–O–C are appear at 530.7 (eV) at 531.9 (eV), respectively, as presented in Fig. 3d [40]. In addition, FT-IR spectroscopy is also used to identify the surface chemistry of dual emissive N-doped CDs, as displayed in Fig. S4. The broadband that appeared at 2700-3550 cm−1 demonstrated that the surface of the obtained N-doped CDs is covered by OH/NH and CH surface functional groups. Strong stretching vibration of HO–C=O at 1770 cm−1 and C=O at 1706 are observed in the IR spectrum. The IR peaks appear at 1590 cm−1 and 1342 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibration of C=C and C=N groups. Moreover, the band centred at 1178 cm−1 has corresponded to the stretching of C–N bond. According to the XPS and FTIR findings, the core structure of the CDs is dominated by numerous nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, which can help to enhance the stability and hydrophilicity of the obtained CDs.

The optical properties of the N-doped CDs are carried out by using UV–Visible absorption and a photo-luminescent spectrometer. The absorbance maxima of the dilute solution of N-doped CDs are observed at 245 nm and 345 nm (Fig. 4 a), which are ascribed to aromatic π–π∗ (C=C) transition and carboxyl n–π∗ (C=O) transition, respectively [41]. In addition, the absorption spectrum shows a broad peak around ∼450 nm, which is related to n-π∗ (C=N/C=O) transition of aromatic bands [22]. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the maxima of PLE spectra are at 360 nm and 470 nm, when the monitoring emission wavelengths are 462 nm and 560 nm, respectively. The PLE result indicates that there are at least two major fluorescent centres on the N-doped CDs.

The resulting N-doped CDs appear yellowish under daylight but emit cyan and yellow light (Fig. 5 a) when exposing to 365 nm UV light and 450 nm blue LED lamp, respectively. Moreover, N-doped CDs droplets have been seen under various laser excitation conditions (see Fig. S5). The N-doped CDs droplet emits in the blue, green and red regions under excitation wavelengths of 406, 488, 561 nm, respectively. The excitation-dependent emission is commonly observed in CDs, but their emission intensity usually shows a large variation (decay) upon changing the excitation wavelengths [23,42]. However, in the present case, when the PLE wavelength is adjusted from 300 nm to 520 nm, the N-doped CDs predominantly display two emission bands, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. Specifically, when the PL excitation varies from 300 to 380 nm, the cyan emission peaking at 462 nm dominated, whereas the excitation wavelength further modulated from 440 to 520 nm, the yellow emission is dominated by the emission peaking at 560 nm. These findings indicate that the produced N-doped CDs have two types of emissive centres, which could be activated by the specific wavelength light. The broad cyan-green emission spectra are recorded, where the PL excitation is modulated from 400 to 420 nm (Fig. 5b). The optimum PL quantum yield is recorded by using an integrated sphere technique. The fluorescent QY of the N-doped CDs is 12.6% and 7.8% under excitation of 360 nm and 470 nm, which present the cyan and yellow emissions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of dual emissive N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the PL mechanism, we have devised a model to explain the dual emission of N-doped CDs based on the existing literature and above results as shown in Fig. 6 [43,44]. We notice large amounts of oxygen (C=O) and nitrogen (C=N) functional groups located on the core surface of the N-doped CDs according to UV–vis, XPS and FT-IR findings. The above functional groups could be responsible for the dual emission of N-doped CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 and Fig. S6, if the excitation wavelength is shorter than 400 nm, the fluorescent centre containing C=O is getting excited and emits cyan light from N-doped CDs. Moreover, the broad emission spectra have been recorded when the excitation wavelength is increased up to 400–420 nm (Fig. S6). This broad (cyan-green emission) somehow corresponds to overlapped oxygen and nitrogen (π∗) states. Furthermore, at lower energy wavelengths from 440 nm to 520 nm, the second fluorescent centre (C=N) absorbs specific wavelength light and emits in the yellow region (Fig. 6 and Fig. S6). As nitrogen plays a significant role in conjugated molecule, n–π∗ (C=N) transition causes a red shift and emit light in the yellow region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of dual emissive N-doped CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Before finding the application of N-doped CDs in bio-system, the photo-stability, pH-dependency, and toxicity assay has been examined. Firstly, we measure the photo-bleaching effect of the obtained N-doped CDs. The resultant N-doped CDs remain stable under continuous UV (8 W lamp) light irradiation for 1 h as displayed in Fig. S7. Secondly, the fluorescent intensity of the N-doped CDs is recorded with different pH values, and it shows very stable performance varied from 2 to 12 (Fig. S8), which is essential for biological applications. Third, the cell viability is measured as demonstrated in Fig. S9. The cytotoxicity of N-doped CDs against T-ca cells is evaluated using a standard CCK8 assay with various doses of N-doped CDs. The result shows that after 1–7 days of incubation with N-doped CDs (3.125–200 μg mL−1), the cell viability is still over 78%. As a result, we can safely utilize 200 μg mL−1 N-doped CDs for cells imaging. The data confirms that the dual emissive N-doped CDs are very stable to different environments (pH, UV irradiation) and have low cytotoxicity, which is very significant for practical applications. Following that, 200 μg mL−1 N-doped CDs are incubated for 4 h with standard conditions at (37 °C with 5% CO2), the incubated T-ca cancer cells are brightly emissive (Fig. 7 ), and their morphologies are observed under a confocal fluorescence microscope. The T-ca cells exhibit blue and green bright fluorescence (Fig. 7a and b) under laser excitation of 406 and 488 nm, respectively. When the excitation wavelength change to 561 nm, T-ca cells illuminate bright red emission, as shown in Fig. 7c. Bright filed merge images of the incubated T-ca cells are presented in Fig. 7d-f. The results reveal that N-doped CDs are mostly found in the cytoplasm and exhibit multicolor fluorescence in response to various laser excitations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term intercellular stability of N-doped CDs in T-ca cells
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, carbon dots perform quite stable in different environments including photo-bleaching and ionic medium as studied above. But their long term biological performance in cancer cells are investigated very rarely. Stick to the point, we examined N-doped CDs intercellular stability for consecutive 8 days (see Fig. 8 ). In detail, N-doped CDs with 200 μg/mL concentration has been incubated to T-ca cells for 6 h. After successfully uptake the cancer cells are washed several times by using PBS solution and then observed the morphology using confocal microscope under various excitation wavelengths. It is clearly be seen in Fig. 8a-c that the T-ca cells emit bright multicolor imaging under various excitation wavelengths after adding fresh medium for 4 h. The corresponding normal bright filed image is displayed in Fig. 8d. The incubated N-doped CDs/T-ca cells are kept for one day under standard condition and then observed their morphology. Fig. 8e-h, show multicolor emission when excited with different excitation wavelengths. Moreover, the N-doped CDs biological stability have been observed after 3rd day as seen in Fig. 8i-l. The N-doped CDs inside the cells still illuminate bright multicolor emission even after 80 h. The relative intercellular fluorescent intensity (see Fig. S10) is recorded and it is still above 50% after keeping them for 80 h in fresh medium. The fluorescence signal drops obviously after 7 days in fresh medium, but intracellular localization fluorescence signal could also be detected as demonstrated in Fig. 8m-p. The zoom area of T-ca cells also exhibit multicolor imaging after different interval of times as presented in Fig. S11. Long term toxicity of N-doped CDs is recorded from 1 to 7 days and the data show 78%–90% viability rate using 200 μg/mL, as confirm from Fig. S9. The results indicates that the as obtained dual emissive N-doped CDs could easily uptake by T-ca cells and emit strong light even after 80 h. N-doped CDs show long term stability performance and it could be promising candidate for drugs release process in future applications.

Building a flexible nano-thermometry device for spatially resolved temperature measurement in biological systems is one of the most important uses of fluorescent CDs. Precise temperature measurements can help to understand inside mechanism of the cell as well as cancer treatment, which could improve the new developments in cancer-biology and other related diseases. Herein, as-obtained dual emissive N-doped CDs are pronounced temperature-dependent emission spectra in both emission ranges (cyan and yellow). The PL peak intensity of N-doped CDs in cyan and yellow emission falls 40% and 38%, respectively, upon varying the experimental temperature from 15 °C to 90 °C as presented in Fig. 9 a. Fig. 9b demonstrate that PL intensity of the dual emissive N-doped CDs fit linearly upon the corresponding temperature for cyan and yellow emission with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9945 and 0.9856, respectively. The average response sensitivity of the dual emissive N-doped CDs is −0.53%/°C for cyan and −0.5%/°C for yellow emission. As we know that, the given temperature range (15–90 °C) is higher than the biological system's required temperature, which illustrates that the as-obtained N-doped CDs have the potential to be applied as temperature sensors in vivo/vitro system. The reason for fluorescence quenching at high temperatures is thermally activated channels such as surface/defect/impurity sites. When the experimental temperature increases, nonradiative channels become thermally activated, which restricts the radiative moment of excitons. As a result, a decline was observed in the PL intensity in both emissions [6].

To further verify that N-doped CDs could be utilized as effective nano-thermal sensors in the living system. T-ca cells are used to investigate thermal sensing phenomenon. The CDs treated T-ca cells are observed with varied temperatures (Fig. 10 ), such as 25 °C, 35 °C, and 45 °C under confocal microscopy. As demonstrated in Fig. 10a-c, the T-ca cells are in good form, well dispersed, and display bright multicolor imaging at room temperature. A weak fluorescent are observed by exposing T-ca cell to high temperature of 35 °C as displayed in Fig. 10d-f. Moreover, at 45 °C the observed T-ca cells show much weaker fluorescence as illustrated in Fig. 10g-i. T-ca cells are of high quality and well separated under the variation of temperature ranging from 25 to 45 °C. Besides, the intracellular PL intensity are calculated under different temperatures (Fig. S12), which show obvious decline in PL intensity accordingly. According to the findings, the obtained N-doped CDs could be used as effective nano-thermal sensors for in vitro imaging. In particular, N-doped CDs might be utilized as dual-mode probes because they allow both intracellular temperature detection and cell imaging. This flexibility signifies another unique advantage of these multimodal nano-probes when compared to other luminescent nanomaterials.

In addition, to further investigate the potential toxicity of N-doped CDs in vivo application for clinical research, the N-doped CDs solution with a concentration of 200 μg/mL and PBS buffer were injected into the kunming mice (n = 5), respectively. The toxicity is studied by collecting heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney slices. The collected slices are stained by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), and then carefully observed there pathological changes in BPS and N-doped CDs groups over span of 30 days. The observed data reveal that there is no obvious histological abnormality in the treated and the control groups as shown in Fig. 11 a. The obtained results clearly indicated that N-doped CDs are high biocompatible and safe enough for in vivo applications. Vivo experiment has been done by using Barker vivo imaging equipment under the 488 nm excitation wavelength. The experiment is carried out on a young Kunming mouse of weight 25 g. The dilute solution of dual emissive N-doped CDs is injected into the bottom of the mice subcutaneously. After 30 min of injection, we observed and recorded the nude mouse under 488 nm excitation with a temperature 27 °C. The N-doped CDs exhibit bright luminescence at the bottom with an average intensity of 5090.5 (a.u), as shown in Fig. 11b. Afterward, the environment temperature change to 35 °C (Fig. 11c), the average intensity of N-doped CDs in Kunming mouse decrease to 5042.7 (a.u). Furthermore, the temperature of the environment slowly increases to 45 °C, as presented in Fig. 11d. The average PL emission intensity is declined to 4983.5 (a.u). In vivo results are also well matching with the above PL temperature-dependent data. All the results are conformed that dual emissive N-doped CDs could be used for in vitro/vivo imaging as well as effective nano-thermometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-doped CDs optical response to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of metal ions on the PL characteristics of the water-soluble N-doped CDs are investigated by measuring the PL intensities in the presence of different metal ions (Ag+, Fe2+, Na+, Mg2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Cr2+, Cu2+) for possible metal sensing application. None of these metal ions has a considerable influence on the fluorescent intensity of the dual emissive N-doped CDs except Fe3+, which induces a decrease in the PL intensity. The corresponding intensity (f/f0) of the dual emissive N-doped CDs in the presence of various metals ions for cyan and yellow emissions are presented in Fig. 12 a and b, respectively. Sensitive probing of Fe3+ remains a big problem in many fields of modern science due to its important involvement in many metabolism processes such as oxygen transportation, oxygen metabolism, DNA synthesis, transfer of electrons, and increased reactivity of catalytic processes [45]. Moreover, the potential capability of the obtained N-doped CDs for quantitative detection of Fe3+ is investigated. PL intensity of the obtained N-doped CDs (cyan and yellow emissions) steadily decreases with increasing concentration of Fe3+ (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, and 300 μM), as shown in Fig. 12c and d, demonstrating that the addition of Fe3+ can effectively quench the luminescence of the obtained N-doped CDs. The strong interaction between hydroxyl or carboxyl groups and Fe3+ causes aggregation of the N-doped CDs in an aqueous medium, which facilitates the recombination of non-radiative excitons through an effective electron transfer process, leading to quenching the fluorescent intensity [17]. For both emissions, the ratios between (f/f0) and Fe3+ concentration exhibit a strong linear connection with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.986 as seen in Fig. 12e and f. Using the 3 times σ/K ratio to calculate the detection limit of the N-doped CDs as a Fe3+ probe is estimated to be 9.3 μM and 15.7 μM for cyan and yellow emission, respectively. The analytical results of recently reported CDs as a sensor for Fe3+ are summarized in Table 1 . The LOD of the obtained N-doped CDs is comparable to the reported CDs. It is essential for practical applications that N-doped CDs could detect Fe3+ in the biological system. In detail, 200 μM concentration of Fe3+ has been incubated with T-ca cells for half an hour, as shown in supporting information Figure S13. The bright fluorescence of the dual emissive N-doped CDs almost disappears in T-ca cells, which indicates the significance of the N-doped CDs for metal ions (Fe3+) detection. The observed findings are consistent with the above results.

We develop for the first time nitrogen-doped carbon dots with dual emission properties using single precursor through one-step and facile solvent-free carbonization. The unique excitation-dependent emission makes these N-doped CDs different from other reported CDs. The N-doped CDs exhibit dual emission with comparable PL intensities. The dual emissive N-doped CDs emit light in the cyan and yellow regions with peak centred at 462 nm 560 nm. The PLQY of obtained N-doped CDs is recorded to be (12.6%, cyan) and (7.8%, yellow) under the excitation of 360 nm and 470 nm. After detailed characterization, oxygen-containing groups (C=O) could be responsible for the cyan emission under high-energy wavelength excitation, while nitrogen-containing groups (C=N) could be responsible for yellow emission under lower energy wavelength excitation. In addition, N-doped CDs have been evaluated in multicolor in vitro, and in vivo imaging, temperature dependent emission and Fe3+ detection. Therefore, we believe that dual emissive N-doped CDs are very promising materials for medical and light display applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this article.
The author declare no compacting conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: W.U.K and P. Z contribute equally to this work. The work was supported by National Natural Science Funds of China grant No# U1905213 and 81571824. Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbkj-2019-ct07, lzujbky-2021-ey14 and lzujbky-2021-kb05), and Lanzhou University Hospital of Stomatology Research Support Fund (LZUKQKY-2019-Y10 and lzukqky-2019-t9). Finally, W.U.K would like to thanks Ge Zhangjie for helping in vivo imaging and provide Kunming mouse.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtnano.2022.100205.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly green emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots with excellent thermal stability for bioimaging and solid-state LED

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.U. Khan, D. Wang, Y. Wang, Highly Green Emissive Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots with Excellent Thermal Stability for Bioimaging and Solid-State LED, Inor. Chem., 57(24) (2018) 15229-15239.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient charge separation and photocurrent generation in the carbon dot-zinc oxide nanoparticle composite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Barman, P. Mitra, R. Bera, S. Das, A. Pramanik, A. Parta, An efficient charge separation and photocurrent generation in the carbon dot-zinc oxide nanoparticle composite, Nanoscale 9(20) (2017) 6791-6799.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facilely synthesized pH-responsive fluorescent polymer dots entrapping doped and coupled doxorubicin for nucleus-targeted chemotherapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Guo, F. Cao, Y. Li, L. Xiong, Facilely synthesized pH-responsive fluorescent polymer dots entrapping doped and coupled doxorubicin for nucleus-targeted chemotherapy, J. Mater. Chem., B 5(16) (2017) 2921-2930.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New development in carbon quantum dots technical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.-M. Shen, J. Liu, New development in carbon quantum dots technical applications, Talanta 156-157 (2016) 245-256.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide and their highly selective detection of lead and pyrophosphate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, Q. Zhou, Y. Yuan, Y. Wu, Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide and their highly selective detection of lead and pyrophosphate, Carbon 115 (2017) 550-560.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble green-emitting carbon nanodots with enhanced thermal stability for biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.U. Khan, L. Qin, A. Alam, P. Zhou, Y. Peng, Y. Wang, Water-soluble green-emitting carbon nanodots with enhanced thermal stability for biological applications, Nanoscale 13(7) (2021) 4301-4307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen, sulfur, boron and flavonoid moiety co-incorporated carbon dots for sensitive fluorescence detection of pesticides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.-K. Li, J.-T. Feng, Z.-Q. Ma, Nitrogen, sulfur, boron and flavonoid moiety co-incorporated carbon dots for sensitive fluorescence detection of pesticides, Carbon 161 (2020) 685-693.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, R. Li, B. Yang, Carbon Dots: A New Type of Carbon-Based Nanomaterial with Wide Applications, ACS Cen. Sci., 6(12) (2020) 2179-2195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insights into photoluminescence mechanisms of carbon dots: advances and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ai, Y. Yang, B. Wang, J. Chang, Z. Tang, B. Yang, S. Lu, Insights into photoluminescence mechanisms of carbon dots: advances and perspectives, Sci. Bull., 66(8) (2021) 839-856.
S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent Carbon Nanodots: Emergent Nanolights, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 49(38) (2010) 6726-6744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Laser irradiated vortex fluidic mediated synthesis of luminescent carbon nanodots under continuous flow

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Luo, A.H.M. Al-Antaki, K. Vimalanathan, J. Moffatt, K. Zheng, Y. Zou, J. Zou, X. Duan, R.N. Lamb, S. Wang, Q. Li, W. Zhang, C.L. Raston, Laser irradiated vortex fluidic mediated synthesis of luminescent carbon nanodots under continuous flow, Reac. Chem. Engineer., 3(2) (2018) 164-170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, Z. Kang, Y. Liu, S.-T. Lee, Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications, J. Mater. Chem., 22(46) (2012) 24230-24253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of bifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from garlic for cellular imaging and free radical scavenging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhao, M. Lan, X. Zhu, H. Xue, T.-W. Ng, X. Meng, C.-S. Lee, P. Wang, W. Zhang, Green Synthesis of Bifunctional Fluorescent Carbon Dots from Garlic for Cellular Imaging and Free Radical Scavenging, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 7(31) (2015) 17054-17060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and large-scale synthesis of green-emitting carbon nanodots from aspartame and the applications for ferric ions sensing and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, Y. Chen, S.-N. Ding, Facile and large-scale synthesis of green-emitting carbon nanodots from aspartame and the applications for ferric ions sensing and cell imaging, Sci. Bull., 62(18) (2017) 1256-1266.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A large-scale synthesis of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots: a self-exothermic reaction driving the formation of the nanocrystalline core at room temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.B. Chen, Z.X. Liu, W.C. Deng, L. Zhan, M.L. Liu, C.Z. Huang, A large-scale synthesis of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots: a self-exothermic reaction driving the formation of the nanocrystalline core at room temperature, Green Chem., 18(19) (2016) 5127-5132.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots—emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Hola, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, E.P. Giannelis, R. Zboril, A.L. Rogach, Carbon dots-Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics, Nano Today 9(5) (2014) 590-603.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.U. Khan, D. Wang, W. Zhang, Z. Tang, X. Ma, X. Ding, S. Du, Y. Wang, High Quantum Yield Green-Emitting Carbon Dots for Fe(ІІІ) Detection, Biocompatible Fluorescent Ink and Cellular Imaging, Sci. Rep., 7(1) (2017) 14866.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, K.R. Aseer, S. Perumal, N. Karthik, Y.R. Lee, Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink, Biosen. Bioelectron., 99 (2018) 303-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable multicolor carbon dots prepared from well-defined polythiophene derivatives and their emission mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Guo, J. Ge, W. Liu, G. Niu, Q. Jia, H. Wang, P. Wang, Tunable multicolor carbon dots prepared from well-defined polythiophene derivatives and their emission mechanism, Nanoscale 8(2) (2016) 729-734.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.-Y. Wang, C.-Y. Chen, C.-M. Wang, Y.Z. Tan, W.-S. Liao, Multicolor Functional Carbon Dots via One-Step Refluxing Synthesis, ACS Sens., 2(3) (2017) 354-363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright multicolor bandgap fluorescent carbon quantum dots for electroluminescent light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yuan, Z. Wang, X. Li, Y. Li, Z.a. Tan, L. Fan, S. Yang, Bright Multicolor Bandgap Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots for Electroluminescent Light-Emitting Diodes, Adv. Mater., 29(3) (2016) 1604436.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Miao, D. Qu, D. Yang, B. Nie, Y. Zhao, H. Fan, Z. Sun, Synthesis of Carbon Dots with Multiple Color Emission by Controlled Graphitization and Surface Functionalization, Adv. Mater., 30(1) (2018) 1704740.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, Q. Hao, Y. Zhang, Y. Xu, W. Lei, Fluorescence quenchometric method for determination of ferric ion using boron-doped carbon dots, Microchim. Acta 183(1) (2016) 273-279.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. He, H. Zhang, J. Zou, Y. Liu, J. Zhuang, Y. Xiao, B. Lei, Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for “off-on” sensing of Hg(II) and I−, Biosen. Bioelectron., 79 (2016) 531-535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterogeneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via anhydrous citric acid and melamine for selective and sensitive turn on-off-on detection of Hg (II), glutathione and its cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Iqbal, K. Iqbal, L. Xu, B. Li, D. Gong, X. Liu, Y. Guo, W. Liu, W. Qin, H. Guo, Heterogeneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via anhydrous citric acid and melamine for selective and sensitive turn on-off-on detection of Hg (II), glutathione and its cellular imaging, Sens. Actuator. B: Chem., 255 (2018) 1130-1138.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A green one-arrow-two-hawks strategy for nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent ink and oxygen reduction electrocatalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Gao, Y. Chen, H. Fan, X. Wei, C. Hu, L. Wang, L. Qu, A green one-arrow-two-hawks strategy for nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent ink and oxygen reduction electrocatalysts, J. Mater. Chem. A 2(18) (2014) 6320-6325.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ge, Q. Jia, W. Liu, M. Lan, B. Zhou, L. Guo, H. Zhou, H. Zhang, Y. Wang, Y. Gu, X. Meng, P. Wang, Carbon Dots with Intrinsic Theranostic Properties for Bioimaging, Red-Light-Triggered Photodynamic/Photothermal Simultaneous Therapy In Vitro and In Vivo, Adv. Healthcare Mater. 5(6) (2016) 665-675.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, S. Perumal, N. Muthuchamy, Y.R. Lee, Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications, Fuel 275 (2020) 117821.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, S. Perumal, R. Vinodh, Y.R. Lee, Betel-derived nitrogen-doped multicolor carbon dots for environmental and biological applications, J. Mol. Liq., 296 (2019) 111817.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S.B. Yu, J.S. Wei, H.M. Xiong, Full-Color Light-Emitting Carbon Dots with a Surface-State-Controlled Luminescence Mechanism, ACS nano 10(1) (2016) 484-491.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hu, A. Trinchi, P. Atkin, I. Cole, Tunable Photoluminescence Across the Entire Visible Spectrum from Carbon Dots Excited by White Light, Angew. Chem. Inter. Ed., 54(10) (2015) 2970-2974.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Red, Green, and Blue Luminescence by Carbon Dots: Full-Color Emission Tuning and Multicolor Cellular Imaging, Angew. Chem. Inter. Ed., 54(18) (2015) 5360-5363.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Ju, H. Nie, Z. Liu, H. Xu, M. Li, C. Wu, H. Wang, S.X.-A. Zhang, Full-colour carbon dots: integration of multiple emission centres into single particles, Nanoscale 9(35) (2017) 13326-13333.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wang, W. U. Khan, Z. Tang, Y. Wang, Applicability Evaluation of Bright Green-Emitting Carbon Dots in the Solid State for White Light-Emitting Diodes, Chem. Asian Jour.,l 13(3) (2018) 292-298.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, J.-S. Wei, H.-M. Xiong, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence, Nanoscale 6(22) (2014) 13817-13823.

Yang, Yellow-emitting carbon-dots-impregnated carboxy methyl cellulose/poly-vinyl-alcohol and chitosan: stable, freestanding, enhanced-quenching Cu2+-ions sensor, J. Mater. Chem. C 6(16) (2018) 4508-4515.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yu, C. Xu, Z. Tian, Y. Lin, Z. Shi, Facilely synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent yield for sensing Fe3+, New J. Chem. 40(3) (2016) 2083-2088.
